 and

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries

 HBsAg

 fluid (C

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.

 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ).  On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints.  Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.

The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female with a known history of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) presented with atraumatic pain and weakness of the left leg. She had previously had a focal neurofibroma excised from the left thigh. MRI imaging of the left femur and knee was performed to identify a cause for the pain.
 Imaging Findings: MRI scan of the left knee (Figures 1, 2, and 3) demonstrated diffuse T2 bright and iso-hypotense T1 tissue infiltrating the subcutaneous and intermuscular planes at the posterior aspect of the knee and reaching the periosteal surface of the distal femur, causing bone dysplasia with widening of the posterior intercondylar notch. CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).

OriginalDescription:

 Imaging Findings: The patient’s MRI brain showed a large lobulated sellar lesion (Figure 1) on post-contrast sagittal images, showing suprasellar extension and anteriorly extending into both sphenoid sinuses and ethmoid sinuses. FLAIR coronal image (Figure 3) shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image (Figure 4), the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Five days after an uncomplicated embolisation of an asymptomatic, growing right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm, a 56-year-old woman comes to the ER due to headache, a seizure and fluctuant left upper extremity weakness and left campimetric defects. A Todd’s paralysis is suspected, and the patient is admitted for a workup.
 Imaging Findings: Initially, a brain CT is performed, which shows no significant abnormalities apart from the embolisation material (Figure 1).
 
 Then a brain MRI is performed, including the following sequences: DWI (Figures 2a and 2b), SWI (Figure 3), T2, FLAIR (Figure 4), 3D TOF and pre and post-gadolinium T1 (Figures 5a and 5b). The MRI discloses multiple round lesions varying in size in the right hemisphere. The lesions are spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, the largest ones showing a small, central T2 hypointensity. Post-gadolinium enhancement is demonstrated in all the lesions. No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artefacts are present.
 
 Follow-up imaging after 6 months (Figures 6, 7, 8a, and 8b) shows overall improvement with resolution of the enhancing foci. However, T2 hyperintensity is still present, and central punctate susceptibility artefacts have appeared, interpreted as microhaemorrhagic foci.

<think>
**Section 1: Candidate Extraction and Precision Mapping**

1. **Non‑ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions** – (c) disease entity; umbrella for various enhancing lesions that are not infarcts.  
2. **Subacute cerebral infarcts** – (c) disease entity; specific imaging phase of ischemic injury.  
3. **Cerebral microabscesses** – (c) disease entity; infectious ring‑enhancing lesions.  
4. **Brain metastases** – (c) disease entity; neoplastic enhancing lesions.

All candidate labels conform to standard nomenclature.

**Section 2: Immediate Pathognomonic Inclusion/Exclusion Sweep**

- **Microabscesses**: Classic finding is restricted diffusion (cytotoxic fluid) – *absent*. Thus “cerebral microabscesses” is definitively excluded.  
- **Brain metastases**: Classic persistence or progression over time; resolution over 6 months is highly atypical – *strong exclusion*.  
- **Subacute infarcts**: Classic post‑gadolinium enhancement >4 days; absence of diffusion restriction after 5 days is expected; **no absolute positive inclusion** that is unique to this diagnosis.  
- **NICE lesions**: No pathognomonic feature present that is unique to this group.  

No single candidate is classically established by an exclusive positive finding, so we proceed to full analysis.

**Section 3: Feature Extraction and Attribution**

| Finding | Label |
|---------|-------|
| Multiple round enhancing lesions primarily in right hemisphere | A (Core) |
| Spontaneous T1 hypointensity, T2 hyperintensity | A (Core) |
| Small central T2 hypointensity in largest lesions | B (Strong Secondary) |
| Absence of restricted diffusion on DWI | A (Core) |
| No susceptibility artefacts on SWI initially | B (Strong Secondary) |
| Resolution of enhancement at 6 months; residual T2 hyperintensity | A (Core) |
| Central punctate susceptibility artefacts (microhaemorrhages) at 6 months | C (Weak Secondary) |

**Section 4: Best‑fit Mechanistic Synthesis, Typicality, and Prioritization**

- **Subacute cerebral infarcts**  
  - *Core supporting evidence*: Post‑gadolinium enhancement in the subacute phase; T2 hyperintensity consistent with ischemic edema; lack of diffusion restriction after ~5 days; multiple small cortical/subcortical lesions explain the embolic origin from aneurysm embolisation; microhaemorrhages develop later due to reperfusion injury.  
  - *Typicality*: Highly typical for multiple small cortical infarcts in the setting of recent endovascular intervention.  
  - *Discriminators*: 1) Timing of enhancement (>4 days). 2) Resolution of enhancement while T2 signal persists. 3) Development of microhaemorrhages.  
  - *Rank*: High‑importance discriminator: resolution of enhancement.

- **Non‑ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions**  
  - *Core supporting evidence*: Enhancing lesions, T2 hyperintensity.  
  - *Typicality*: Possible but variant (NICE is a heterogeneous umbrella). No mandatory pathology (e.g., demyelination, infection) found.  
  - *Discriminators*: 1) Absence of diffusion restriction (fits, but many diseases in NICE also lack it). 2) Rapid resolution of enhancement (less common in demyelinating or inflammatory processes; usually persists if untreated).  
  - *Rank*: Secondary discriminator; less convincing due to resolution pattern.

- **Brain metastases**  
  - *Core supporting evidence*: Enhancing lesions; T2 hyperintensity.  
  - *Typicality*: Rare; metastases usually persist or progress, not resolve spontaneously in 6 months.  
  - *Discriminators*: 1) Rapid resolution of enhancement – highly atypical.  
  - *Rank*: Low likelihood; strong exclusion.

- **Cerebral microabscesses**  
  - *Core supporting evidence*: Enhancing lesions.  
  - *Typicality*: Rare; microabscesses exhibit restricted diffusion – absent here.  
  - *Discriminators*: 1) Diffusion restriction – missing.  
  - *Rank*: Excluded.

**Section 5: Explicit Negative Reasoning / Exclusion Table**

| Candidate | Exclusion Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Cerebral microabscesses | Missing diffusion restriction; T2 central hyperintensity would normally be hypointense. |
| Brain metastases | Resolution of enhancement within 6 months; absence of mass effect or progression. |
| Non‑ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions | Rapid resolution of contrast enhancement unlikely in demyelinating or inflammatory entities; no evidence of underlying disease process. |

**Section 6: Single‑label Selection/Abstain**

Only “Subacute cerebral infarcts” coherently explains all core features (multiple enhancing lesions, absence of diffusion, temporal evolution) and matches typical pattern for postoperative embolic phenomena. No other candidate surpasses its specificity and typicality.  
Therefore, the final diagnosis is **Subacute cerebral infarcts**.

</think>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old female presented with a four-day history of sudden onset frontal headache, vomiting, ophthalmalgia, photophobia and blurred vision. Her past medical history included migraines, right-sided phacoemulsion and an intraocular lens implant six years ago. Fundoscopy demonstrated choroidal folds and effusions. Laboratory blood workup revealed only elevated serum ACE levels.
 Imaging Findings: Initial computerised tomography (CT) head imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head were unremarkable. Specifically, CT did not identify any orbital pathology. MRI of the orbits revealed bilateral effusions in the suprachoroidal space, with low signal intensity on T1 sequences (Figure 1) and high-intensity signal on T2 sequences (Figure 2). Findings were more pronounced on the right side. Effusions extended anteriorly past the ora serrata. Contrast-enhanced sequences with fat suppression visualised choroidal and scleral enhancement (Figure 3). There was no evidence of intraocular mass or retinal detachment.
 
 A subsequent high-resolution CT chest scan revealed biapical scarring, mild bronchial wall thickening and subtle fissure nodularity in the upper zones (Figure 4) and a 10 mm subcarinal lymph node (Figure 5). Thoracic imaging findings were non-specific and equivocal for sarcoidosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy presented with pain over the left hip for the last 3 years and a gradually enlarging mass for the last 2 years. Upon physical examination, the left hip range of motion was reduced. Lab tests showed normal calcium, high serum phosphorous of 7.07 Eq/dL, and normal parathormone levels.
 Imaging Findings: A radiograph of the left hip demonstrated multilobulated periarticular calcifications (Figure 1). MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) showed a well-defined, lobulated lesion affecting the glutei, vastus lateralis, and obturator externus muscles. Multiple fluid-calcium levels, termed the “sedimentation sign”, were noted, with calcifications appearing hypointense on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences, and fluid appearing hypointense on T1-weighted and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Histopathological examination (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed the diagnosis of tumoral calcinosis, revealing fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications arranged in lobules with surrounding multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, mild jaundice and dark urine for 3 days. Initial blood tests revealed anaemia and elevation of serum creatinine, total bilirubin, and lactate dehydrogenase. Further analytical evaluation excluded autoimmune and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemias. Flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood showed a deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins. Abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any significant findings, so an abdominal magnetic resonance (MRI) was subsequently conducted.
 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 (Figure 1) and T1-weighted (Figure 2) sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence (Figure 3) compared to the in-phase sequence (Figure 2). Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c). The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities (Figure 5). Isolated renal haemosiderosis was presumed.
 
 Abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography did not reveal significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 26-year-old female with no relevant past medical history presented with a two-year history of worsening pain at the right proximal humerus and associated weakness.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the right shoulder (Figures 1a and 1b) showed an expansile lucent proximal humeral meta-diaphysis lesion with well-defined medial and craniocaudal margins, narrow zone of transition, mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
 
 CT of the right shoulder (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass and an extensive area of full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly with focal permeation of the posterior cortex of the meta-diaphysis.
 
 Subsequent MRI (Figures 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) confirmed a 4.8 cm maximum diameter predominantly fat-signal lesion within the proximal humerus with mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding and more nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat and extensive full thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation with secondary stress fracture. Mildly enhancing mild peri-lesional marrow and cortical oedema were also noted. There was no extra-osseous invasion.
 
 Nuclear medicine whole-body scan revealed avid uptake at the right proximal humerus with no other disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male with a history of traumatic brain injury as an infant with subsequent hydrocephalus status post ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt. Shunt revision was done 2 years back. Elevated blood pressure was noted in the outpatient department and referred to emergency.
 Imaging Findings: CT head demonstrated ventriculomegaly (Figure 1a) compared to the baseline CT one year back (Figure 1b).
 
 The shunt series, including anteroposterior radiographs of the skull, neck, chest, and abdomen, showed a left-sided VP shunt coursing along the neck and chest with the peritoneal part coiled within the right upper quadrant. A large globular density was noted in the right upper quadrant overlying the liver shadow. Prior abandoned shunt is also visualised (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The right upper quadrant ultrasound showed a fluid collection displacing the liver posteriorly (Figure 3).
 
 CT abdomen demonstrated a subcapsular collection of fluid with scalloping of the liver parenchyma. The distal shunt coiled and terminated within the cyst (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old male patient with a history of alcoholism and smoking was referred to the otorhinolaryngology department for dysphagia. Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. We performed an FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated an intensely hypermetabolic uvula (SUVmax at 9.2), with moderate hypermetabolism of the adenopathies (SUVmax at 2.5) and the liver lesion (SUVmax at 5.4) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. A clockwise whirl sign composed of spiralled loops of collapsed cecum and ileum, with twisting of central fatty mesentery with enhancing distended vessels, was present (Figures 4a and 4b). The gradual tapering of the obstructed cecum at the site of the whirl resulted in a stenosis, with the appearance of a bird’s beak (Figure 5). There was also dilation of small bowel loops, filled with fluid. There were no signs of bowel perforation, as well as no signs of pneumatosis intestinalis or other signs of vascular compromise.

On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening (Figure 1a) and bilateral hydronephrosis.
 
 CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity” (Figures 1b and 1c). Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old patient is referred from the primary care due to epigastric pain radiating to the left flank, accompanied by asthenia and episodes of postprandial nausea, as well as signs of panniculitis in both lower limbs. Blood tests revealed elevated CA 19-9 levels (135.5 U/mL).
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed, revealing a hypodense lesion with cystic appearance in contact with the anteroinferior margin of the pancreatic tail, with areas of higher density inside it, which are suggestive of septations (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 A contrast-enhanced MRI was conducted to further characterise the lesion. It showed a heterogeneous tumour with an atypical cystic appearance, related to higher signal intensity on T1-weighted and lower intensity on T2-weighted images, when compared to free water and the presence of small cystic rounded areas with higher intensity on T2-weighted images (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Some small foci of signal drop on out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences compared with in-phase T1-weighted sequences were observed inside the lesion (Figures 3a and 3b). Additionally, areas of diffusion restriction were noted within the lesion (Figures 3c and 3d) and contrast-enhancing septations (Figure 3e). There was no visible communication with the main pancreatic duct.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old male with progressive imbalance while walking and generalised tonic-clonic seizures from 5 years of age. No significant family history. Neurological examination revealed lower limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, extensor plantar reflex, features of ataxia, impaired pain, and touch sensations. Ophthalmic and fundus examinations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed the atrophy of the superior cerebellar vermis and the atrophy of the superior cerebellar cortex. The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared (Figures 2 and 3). T2/FLAIR linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncle giving a tigroid/striped appearance (Figures 1a and 1b). Atrophy of the cervical and thoracic spinal cord is seen (Figure 3). Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri (Figure 4) with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum (Figure 2) also seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old woman with a history of controlled asthma and splenectomy following a gunshot wound 30 years prior presented with a dry cough, occasionally producing yellow-green sputum. She reported intermittent moderate dyspnoea and wheezing on auscultation, with symptoms gradually worsening over the past 30 days.
 Imaging Findings: Chest computed tomography (CCT) revealed solid nodules with homogeneous attenuation and enhancement (Figure 1), smooth borders, no calcifications (Figure 2), and no distortion of the adjacent lung parenchyma within the left pleural space (Figure 3). The nodules were randomly distributed along the longitudinal axis and measured up to 5.9 cm at the base of the hemithorax (Figure 4). A previous splenectomy was confirmed by the absence of the spleen in the abdominal cavity (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 76-year-old male patient with a prior diagnosis of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) was referred to the emergency department from a nursing home due to progressive clinical deterioration over the past 24 hours. The patient presented decreased consciousness. No fever or recent trauma was reported. An urgent CT scan was requested (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT performed four years earlier at the onset of CAA (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed an isolated convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) in the right frontal sulcus without trauma, in a patient with mild cognitive impairment.
 
 In the MRI one month after SAH, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequence (Figure 2), significant superficial haemosiderosis and multiple supra and infratentorial microhaemorrhages are observed as hypointense areas with a “blooming” effect.
 
 Urgent CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 4) demonstrated an intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia, a smaller left frontal haemorrhage, and extensive hypodensity in the left supratentorial white matter suggestive of cerebral oedema.
 
 In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c) and FLAIR (Figures 5d and 5e) sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction (Figures 5f and 5g), corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 
 CT one month after the acute event (Figures 6a, 6b, 6c, 6d and 7) showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 68-year-old male, a known case of chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage V on dialysis, complaints of swelling in the left forearm and difficulty in cannulation. The patient had a history of left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula formation with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) brachiocephalic graft. Local examination showed dilated tortuous swellings on the left forearm reaching up to the cubital fossa.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination showed a relatively well-defined, predominantly hypoechoic lesion with a cystic component within, showing multiple septations and communication to the underlying vessel.
 
 Colour Doppler examination showed a typical “to-and-fro” pattern of flow (yin-yang sign) within the lesion and vessel with turbulent flow adjacent to the lesion. Communication with flow was noted between the lesion and the adjacent vessel.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks of gestation presented with a growing, painless mass in her left breast. The patient had no significant past medical history or family history of breast cancer. Clinical examination revealed a firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the retro-areolar region of the left breast.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging at 12 weeks gestation showed a well-circumscribed lobulated heterogeneous mass with predominantly isoechoic echogenicity containing hyperechoic areas. No increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound was noted. These features could suggest a benign mass with lactational changes (e.g., fibroadenoma, hamartoma, or galactocele) (BI-RADS category 3) (Figures 1a and 1b). At 24 weeks, the lesion grew to 100×53×114 mm (Figures 2a and 2b). At 38 weeks of gestation, the mass was occupying the whole breast. The increased vascularity on colour Doppler ultrasound is most likely due to breast engorgement during pregnancy (Figure 3). A core needle biopsy confirmed the lesion as benign with no evidence of malignancy (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old male presents with a history of microscopic haematuria, a palpable right flank mass, and recurrent headaches. A CT scan was performed, followed by a brain MRI to rule out tumoural pathology. The patient had no significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: In the abdominal CT scan, multiple bilateral renal cystic lesions with septum formation were observed. The right kidney had multiple, irregular, heterogeneous lesions with significant enhancement post-contrast, and hypodense central areas related to necrosis, the biggest located in the superior pole. Similar characteristics were noted in the left kidney lesions (Figure 1). Also, multiple simple pancreatic cysts and splenic vein dilation were observed, along with pancreaticosplenic collaterals. There is a lesion in the splenic hilum that enhances and follows contrast in different phases, suggestive of a splenic hamartoma (Figure 2).
 
 The brain MRI revealed multiple heterogeneous lesions with a cystic and a solid component in the posterior fossa, showing avid and homogeneous enhancement of the solid portion, and causing vasogenic oedema and mass effect on the pons (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old woman presented to an otorhinolaryngology consultation with a 2-month history of dysphonia and a 2-year history of gradual right hearing loss. No other symptoms were reported. Right vocal cord paralysis was noted at flexible nasofibrolaryngoscopy. A CT of the neck and chest was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT demonstrated signs of right vocal cord paralysis, right tongue haemiatrophy, and atrophy of the right trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles (Figure 1). A lytic lesion in the right skull base was noted, only partially covered, given the undirected protocol. There were no other relevant findings.
 
 A brain and skull base CT and MRI were requested for further evaluation. CT showed a lytic lesion centred at the petroclival synchondrosis, with extensive destruction of the clivus and petrous temporal bone, showing calcified areas and involvement of the foramen jugular and carotid canal (Figure 2).
 
 At MRI, the lesion showed marked T2WI hyperintensity, T1WI hypointensity, and heterogeneous gadolinium enhancement (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). There was no restriction to the diffusion of water molecules, with high ADC values (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 The lesion infiltrated the right cavernous sinus, right Meckel’s cave, foramen rotundum, foramen ovale, and the hypoglossal canal (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male driver presented with insidious onset pain and effusion of the right knee for 3 weeks. The patient had a similar history the previous year, which resolved after taking nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). The knee range of motion was terminally painful. There was no history of trauma, arthritis or comorbidities.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the right knee revealed normal bone density without fracture or erosion. MRI of the right knee revealed patchy areas in the epimetaphyseal region of both the femoral condyles, showing hyperintense signals on proton density fat-suppressed (PDFS) images (Figure 1) and correlating hypointense signals on T1W images (Figure 2), suggestive of marrow oedema. Mild knee joint effusion was noted without synovial thickening, erosions or low signal lines. The ligaments and menisci were normal. The tibial condyles, fibula and patella showed normal marrow signal intensity. The articular cartilage, posteromedial and posterolateral corners of the knee joint were normal.
