OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented to the paediatric department with a history of headache, which was severe and diffuse in nature and associated with 2–3 episodes of non-projectile vomiting. There was no associated history of blurring of vision, loss of consciousness, seizure, trauma, fever, pain abdomen or loose stool. On examination, he had an emaciated appearance with microcephaly and hyperextensible joints.
 
 The patient had developmental delay since infancy, which he gradually gained at >3 years of age. He had an episode of seizure at 6 years of age, and his EEG revealed generalised epileptiform discharges. He was started on valproate, which was stopped in 2020. He continued to have multiple episodes of headache and vomiting during this period. He presented with bilateral papilledema in September 2021.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed T2 hyperintensities (Figures 1a and 1b) involving bilateral subcortical, deep, periventricular and cerebellar white matter with sparing of the perirolandic white matter. These areas show restriction of diffusion (Figures 2a and 2b) and do not show blooming on gradient echo (GRE) images. There is a prominence of bilateral optic nerves with tortuosity and optic nerve sheath dilatation (Figures 3a and 3b) with mild subluxation of both lenses (Figure 4). There is a relative increase in choline creatinine peak with preserved N-acetylaspartate (NAA) in TE 135 ms (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with 2 days of severe right-sided lower abdominal pain. He was pyrexial with a raised neutrophil count (9.2x10^9/L) and CRP (55mg/L). Examination revealed right inguinal swelling and tenderness. Due to the high suspicion of incarcerated inguinal hernia, a CT abdomen and pelvis was requested.
 Imaging Findings: A portal venous phase CT abdomen and pelvis found a thickened and hyper-enhancing right spermatic cord, with the supra-scrotal vas deferens measuring up to 5mm. Further inflammation was seen within the inguinal canal, and a small reactive hydrocele was noted. A unilaterally enlarged right seminal vesicle was identified, however, there was no abscess.
 
 On the assumption that these findings were due to infection, oral ciprofloxacin was commenced, with temperatures and pain settling within the first 24 hours. The patient has not had any subsequent presentations. A right inguinal hernia repair was also performed during the inpatient stay with antibiotic cover to reduce the risk of infection. There were no complications.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old male patient presented with right-sided limb weakness and slurred speech for a month. No vomiting/seizures/syncope was reported.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a well-defined lesion in the left periventricular region, measuring 1.8 x 1.5 x 1.6 cm (AP x ML x CC) with significant perifocal oedema (Figure 1). The lesion was T2 hetero-intense (Figure 1) with a T2 hypointense and T1 hyperintense rim (Figure 2), showing diffusion restriction (Figure 4). Associated mass effects noted were midline shift to the right, left uncal herniation, and effacement of the left lateral ventricle (Figure 6). Extensive vasogenic oedema was noted involving the left gangliocapsular region, left frontotemporal lobe, left hemi-midbrain, and left hemi-pons (Figure 1). On post-contrast images, the lesion showed heterogenous enhancement with involvement of the left lateral ventricular margin (Figure 3).
 
 Tractography showed interruption of white matter tracts in the region of the lesion (Figure 5a). The region of perilesional oedema showed only deviation of the left internal capsule and left superior longitudinal fasciculus tracts (Figure 5a), explaining the patient’s symptoms. Fractional anisotropy was decreased (Figure 5b) while mean diffusivity was increased (Figure 5c) within the lesion and in the region of perilesional oedema.
 
 The patient then underwent left FP craniotomy and excision of the lesion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman came to the emergency department with complaints of multiple bone pains. She reported fractures of the right forearm and the right leg three years ago, occurring without any significant trauma. Laboratory findings revealed elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP > 1400) and parathyroid hormone (720 pg/ml), serum calcium level was 10.8 mg/dl, and serum creatinine level was 0.9. There was no skin lesion.
 Imaging Findings: Skeletal Survey (Radiograph of the skull, dorso-lumbar spine and both upper and lower extremities)
 
 The shafts of the long bones showed ground glass opacities with expansion of the medullary cavity and thinning of the cortex. There were multiple lytic lesions in the shaft of the long bones, and a few of them showed a sclerotic rim. There were areas of sclerosis in the metaphysis of the long bones (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The dorsal lumbar spine showed reduced bone density with endplate sclerosis, consistent with a rugger-jersey spine (Figure 2b). Multiple lytic lesions were seen in the scapula, ribs, clavicle, multiple vertebral bodies, pelvic bones and metacarpals on both sides (Figures 2a and 2c). There was subperiosteal resorption on the radial aspect of the head of the second proximal phalanx (Figure 3). Skull radiograph showed a widening of the diploic spaces with multiple well-defined, variable-sized sclerotic lesions (Figure 4).
 
 CT Brain
 
 The CT brain revealed a widening of diploic spaces with multiple well-defined sclerotic and ground glass density lesions (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c).
 
 USG Neck
 
 In the US of the neck, there was a well-defined heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid (Figure 6).
 
 CT Parathyroid Protocol
 
 A hypodense enhancing lesion posterior to the right lobe of the thyroid was detected, suggestive of parathyroid adenoma (Figures 7a, 7b, 8a, 8b, 9a, 9b, 9c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old woman presented with permanent pain of increasing intensity in her right leg that had been evolving for 24 hours. The pain appeared immediately following a horse ride in an improperly adjusted stirrup and was not relieved by paracetamol, ketoprofen and morphine. No significant medical history.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the legs showed a swollen appearance of the right anterolateral compartment muscles with:
 
 Fat-suppressed coronal T2-weighted (Figure 1) and axial PD-SPIR (Figure 2) emphasised the acute involvement of the lateral compartment of the leg through a noticeable bulging of the compartment muscles, which is clearly visible in the axial view when compared to the healthy contralateral side. Additional loss of fascicular architecture is seen. Diffuse hyperintensity of the compartment indicates muscular oedema.
 
 Sagittal T1-weighted sequence showed slight spontaneous high signal intensity within the involved muscles (Figure 3).
 
 After injection, the fat-suppressed axial T1-weighted sequence (Figure 4) showed focal defects of enhancement within the proximal portion of the muscles, indicating areas of ischemia.
 
 No other causes of acute pain, including a stress fracture, deep vein thrombosis or focal muscular strain, were identified on the examination. The diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome was confirmed by MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old woman presents with a left preauricular lump of two weeks’ evolution. Physical examination reveals a soft, mobile 1 cm left preauricular lump upon superficial palpation. An MRI and a fine-needle aspiration biopsy was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Facial MRI revealed a small lesion in the superficial lobe of the left parotid gland, located cranial to the Stensen duct, and anterior and lateral to the facial nerve, showing mild heterogeneity and peripheral enhancement after contrast injection. It exhibited a very low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) value on the ADC map (0.4 to 0.5x10^-3 mm
 
 2
 
 /second) and a type III TIC (time-intensity curve), suggesting radiological aggressiveness (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e).
 
 Additionally, a marked enlargement of the left lacrimal gland was observed, with similar radiological findings on diffusion and dynamic gadolinium-enhancement sequences, indicative of an infiltrative process (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e).
 
 The low ADC values on both lesions gave the clue to diagnose a possible concomitant lymphoproliferative process, and this finding is key to the diagnosis [1].

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female presented to the casualty with an episode of loss of consciousness for a period of thirty minutes. After regaining consciousness, the patient lost power in all four limbs and was unable to walk without assistance.
 Imaging Findings: On NCCT (non-contrast computed tomography) brain, multiple small hypodense cystic lesions were noted, many showing scolex inside depicted by the hyperdense eccentric dot within and surrounding oedema (Figures 1a and 1b). A few of them were seen with calcifications inside, suggesting calcified scolices, thus describing the different stages of the disease. A sub-centimetric calcified lesion was seen in the intermuscular plane between the left semispinalis capitis and trapezius muscle (Figures 1a and 1b). On MRI correlation, multiple well-defined sub-centimetric hyperintense lesions were noted in bilateral cerebral hemispheres. Internal eccentric scolex was noted; however, no evidence of mass effect was seen (Figures 2a and 2b). On X-ray examination, multiple calcified lesions were seen in the intermuscular plane along bilateral proximal lower limbs, suggesting disseminated disease and rice grain calcifications (Figure 3). Further ultrasonographic evaluation depicted a non-compressible, hypoechoic cystic lesion with a central calcification in the gracilis muscle along the medial compartment of the thigh (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female with a history of hypertension, lower back pain from multiple older compression fractures and long-term hyponatraemia was referred for a thoracic and abdominal CT scan with contrast to rule out malignancy. The present case reports an incidental finding with no correlation to the abovementioned symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast CT showed a local, rounded, well-defined mass measuring 30 mm x 52 mm x 41 mm (AP x CC x TR), with a low-density fat component, a soft tissue component and no cortical erosion, destruction or scalloping (Figures 1, 2, and 3). In close proximity to the mass, a marginally enlarged lymph node was located cranially to the left of the mass (Figure 3).
 
 MRI with T1-weighted (T1W) (Figure 4) and STIR (Figure 5) images showed the presacral mass to have a high heterogeneous signal, but with a fat signal, and the mass to be well defined and with high probability of being encapsulated. Additionally, T1-fat saturation weighted sequence without and with contrast (Figures 6, 7, and 8) showed the mass to be of high fat content, showing discrete linear strands of contrast enhancement in the mass, but no nodular enhancement. MRI also confirmed no presence of bone marrow oedema.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old female patient presented with exertional dyspnoea, weakness, abdominal pain, and lower limb swelling persisting for a month. There was no history of fever. Initial ultrasound investigation revealed mild ascites, multiple small abdominal lymphadenopathies and minimal pericardial effusion. Laboratory results showed haemoglobin of 12.2g/dL, platelet count of 105×109/L and white blood cell count of 3.32×10
 
 9
 
 /L. A 12-lead ECG revealed prolonged P waves, abnormal Q waves, poor R wave progression, and T wave abnormalities (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography revealed mild pericardial effusion, grade II diastolic dysfunction, right ventricle with segmental aneurysmal dilation and apical retraction, mild mitral regurgitation, and hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex, indicative of fibrofatty tissue deposition. While the right ventricle (RV) showed localised enlargement due to aneurysm formation, the overall restrictive physiology remained evident (Figures 2a and 2b). No thrombus was detected on echocardiography and cardiac MRI. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and elevated diaphragms.
 
 The patient was referred for a cardiac MRI in our department. Cardiac MRI, done with cine imaging, perfusion, and early and late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) PSIR sequences on MAGNETOM Vida 3T scanner revealed ascites, pericardial effusion, enlarged right atrium, retraction of the RV apex (RV apical notch), RV aneurysm, obliteration of the left ventricular (LV) apex on True FISP (true fast imaging with steady-state free precession) cine imaging (Figure 3). The LV end-diastolic volume was 69 mL, and the end-systolic volume was 28 mL. Similarly, the RV end-diastolic volume measured 70 mL, with an end-systolic volume of 39 mL, indicating relatively small chamber volumes. LGE imaging demonstrated subendocardial fibrosis with fat infiltration at both the LV and RV apices, resulting in a V-shaped enhancement pattern in each ventricle (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d).

 Schistosoma mansoni
 
 . An ultrasound and a CT scan were then ordered.

Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy was brought by his parents with complaints of pain and swelling in the right hip and right iliac fossa since three months. He had difficulty in walking since one month. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the right hip.
 Imaging Findings: A pelvic radiograph revealed a soft tissue opacity in the right iliac fossa, erosion of the right sacroiliac (SI) joint and the right sacral ala, with the right hip joint abducted and externally rotated (Figure 1).
 
 CT demonstrated a heterogeneously hypodense lesion originating from the right sacrum, with a large exophytic pelvic component and soft tissue extension into the right SI joint (Figures 2a and 2b), displacing bowel loops and the bladder to the left (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 MRI revealed a T1/T2 heterogeneously hyperintense soft tissue lesion from the right sacrum with an exophytic pelvic component and multiple cystic areas suggesting blood degradation products (Figures 4a and 4b). There were diffusion-restricted areas (Figures 5a and 5b), extension into the S1 and S2 neural foramina with nerve root encasement (Figure 6), and heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement (Figure 7).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented to the medical OPD with complaints of ataxia, multiple episodes of falls while walking, and bed wetting for a one-year duration, with gradually progressive symptoms. On examination, the patient had shuffling gait, tremors, and cogwheel rigidity. Nerve conduction studies, parathyroid hormone assay and calcium studies were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT head revealed bilaterally symmetrical hyperdense areas in bilateral basal ganglia, thalami, subcortical white matter, red nucleus, dentate nucleus and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 MRI brain revealed bilaterally symmetrical areas that were hyperintense on T1WI, hypointense on T2WI, and showing blooming on gradient echo sequences (GRE) images in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalami (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c), bilateral red nuclei (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), bilateral dentate nuclei and cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c), and subcortical white matter (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). These areas did not show restriction of diffusion on DWI images. There were discrete and confluent areas of T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in periventricular and deep white matter of bilateral frontoparietal lobes, showing no restriction of diffusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male patient with no significant past medical background is referred for left knee pain since the age of 10, associated with knee swelling. Since then, he has always maintained complaints but lived a normal life. Plain radiograph, CT scan and MR were ordered for further investigation.
 Imaging Findings: On plain film (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d), there are periosteal and endosteal cortical thickening associated with extra-cortical bone formation along the anterior aspect of the left tibia and the medial aspect of the left femur. Areas of central medullary hyperostosis can be observed in the inferior third of the femoral diaphysis and in the femoral head. The lesions have an appearance that resembles “flowing candle wax”.
 
 CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e) allows for further acknowledgement of the changes described above.
 
 MR performed on a 1.5T MR scanner (T1 and STIR sequences in axial and coronal planes; Figures 3a and 3b) depict extensive changes with very low signal on all imaging sequences, mainly involving the left femur and tibia, with diffuse thickening of the bone cortex giving the bone surface a wavy appearance, as well as multiple intramedullary foci showing marked hypointensity, indicating a strong component of bone sclerosis. The lesions show eccentric growth, extending from the diaphysis to the epiphysis, and exceeding the cortical surface.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old woman (P1L1) presented with a two‐day history of acute pelvic pain and vomiting. There was no history of fever or vaginal bleeding. Her menstrual cycles were regular, but often accompanied by mild dysmenorrhea. No history of tubal ligation. The patient’s medical history was unremarkable, and a urinary pregnancy test was negative.
 Imaging Findings: Transvaginal sonography (TVS) revealed a convoluted, heterogeneously echogenic mass in the pouch of Douglas (POD), predominantly towards the right, showing significant surrounding inflammation. At its distal end was a well-defined, hypoechoic cystic lesion measuring approximately 3×4 cm, exhibiting peripheral mucosal infoldings with a classic cog-wheel pattern, strongly indicating tubal origin. The cyst contained homogeneous low-level internal echoes, raising suspicion for a haematosalpinx.
 
 Doppler imaging demonstrated a twisted vascular pedicle with a near-classic whirlpool pattern, along with pronounced adnexal hyperaemia. The right ovary maintained its normal anatomical position, morphology and good stromal vascularity, however, with mild periovarian hyperaemia. A small volume of echogenic free fluid was present in the POD.
 
 The left ovary, adnexa, uterus and cervix appeared unremarkable, while the endometrium exhibited features of hyperplasia.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old non-obese female with a history of cholecystectomy in the last four months presents to the emergency department with clinical signs including right upper quadrant pain, generalised jaundice, and a fever of 38.5°C. Liver function tests reveal hepatic cytolysis and cholestasis (elevated AST/ALT and ALP). An abdominal ultrasound was requested.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound with a low-frequency probe reveals the presence of multiple hyperechoic formations within the intrahepatic bile ducts, causing posterior acoustic shadowing (Figure 1) and resulting in dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 2 and 3). The common bile duct was clear.
 
 The presence of jaundice and fever raised concerns about an associated cholangitis, prompting the indication for hepatobiliary MRI. The MRI reveals more pronounced dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts, particularly in the left lobe of the liver, containing multiple signal-void formations consistent with the previously described biliary calculi (Figures 4 and 5). Additionally, T1-weighted sequences post-gadolinium demonstrate subtle thickening and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls along with peribiliary hepatic infiltration, secondary to the inflammation of the liver adjacent to biliary ducts caused by potential cholangitis (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 53-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with an episode of dysarthria followed by a decrease in the level of consciousness, without sphincter involvement. Physical examination upon admission was unremarkable, with no signs of neurological focal deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced CT shows two hypodense frontal lesions involving the cortex and the subcortical white matter with minimal surrounding oedema (Figure 1). The right lesion presents with a gyriform coarse calcification, and there is no enhancement after intravenous contrast administration (Figure 2). The left lesion produces light bone remodelling.
 
 On MRI, the lesions exhibit heterogeneous signal intensity and ill-defined margins, predominantly showing hyperintensity on T2/FLAIR sequences without communication between them through the corpus callosum (Figures 3a and 3b), and areas of hypointensity on susceptibility-weighted imaging consistent with coarse calcifications on the right side and bleeding on the left side (Figures 4a and 4b). There is no significant diffusion restriction (Figures 5a and 5b). Following contrast administration, only minimal linear enhancement is observed on both lesions (Figure 6).
 
 A perfusion study was carried out showing an increase in the cerebral blood volume (Figures 7a and 7b), and spectroscopy reveals a decrease in the N-acetylaspartate (N-Ace) peak, no elevation of lactate (LL) and an increased choline (Ch) level (Figures 8a and 8b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old woman presented with epigastric pain and uneasiness since six months. She had no difficulty in swallowing or regurgitation of food. She had no respiratory complaints. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was unremarkable. The patient was subjected to an erect chest radiograph for routine evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Erect frontal chest radiograph showed a well-defined, lobulated, homogeneous, soft tissue opacity (green arrow) in the left lower hemithorax, obscuring the medial aspect of the left dome of the diaphragm. It was seen as continuous with an ill-defined, left paravertebral soft tissue opacity (pink arrow). There was no silhouetting of the left heart border (Figure 1).
 
 A barium (95% w/v) oesophagogram showed unobstructed passage of barium through the thoracic soft tissue opacity. The retrocardiac oesophagus was tortuous and deviated to the left with a normal gastro-oesophageal junction (Figure 2).
 
 An unenhanced CT of the chest was performed with oral contrast, followed by intravenous contrast-enhanced CT with oral contrast in the same sitting. Axial unenhanced images showed circumferential, homogeneous thickening of the lower oesophageal wall with patency of the lumen showing orally administered water-soluble contrast. There were a few tiny foci of calcification within the wall (pink arrow on Figure 3). Sagittal venous phase images showed homogeneous enhancement of the thickened oesophageal wall without areas of necrosis (blue arrow) or evidence of invasion into surrounding structures (Figure 4). Coronal unenhanced images in the lung window showed surrounding atelectasis without signs of lung parenchymal involvement (Figure 5).

 Additional CT thorax and abdomen, PET-CT, and whole body MRI showed no other lesions or adenopathies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 2). Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve (Figure 4), following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head (Figures 5a and 5b). Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old female presented with a 3-day history of upper abdominal pain and nausea without vomiting, bowel changes, or systemic symptoms. Examination revealed epigastric tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory results showed mild elevated CRP (16 mg/L) and WCC (11.9 x 10⁹/L), with normal liver functions and amylase levels.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed ill-defined fat stranding in the lesser omentum, located near the lesser curvature of the stomach. This fat stranding was not associated with a well-defined mass. The stomach and pancreas appeared normal on imaging, except for a small hiatus hernia identified incidentally. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal gas, bowel wall thickening, or signs of bowel obstruction. No other abnormalities were observed in the abdominal and pelvic structures, and no additional masses or lesions were noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old male presented with pain in the right hypochondrium and loss of appetite for 1 month. There was no history of fever. The patient has a history of a successfully treated abdominal tuberculosis, for which he had taken anti-tubercular treatment for 1 year.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound was performed, which showed a well-defined round to oval isoechoic lesion in segment IV of the liver, without any significant internal vascularity.
 
 A contrast CT scan was further requested, which demonstrated a well-defined round to oval lesion, measuring ~6 x 4.2 x 5.1 cm, well demarcated from the rest of the liver with the same attenuation as that of the normal liver tissue. It was located inferior to segment IV (Figures 1a and 1b) with arterial supply from a branch of the left hepatic artery (Figure 2) and venous drainage into a branch of the middle hepatic vein. It was seen to cause mild dilatation of central and peripheral intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe due to compression (Figure 3). The liver was otherwise normal in size with maintained segmental morphology and normal hepatic veins. The left branch of the portal vein was attenuated in calibre; however, it showed normal contrast opacification (Figure 4). Physiological areas of altered attenuation were also noted in the right lobe of the liver.

1. **Accessory liver lobe**

   - *Typicality:* Highly typical/classic for a well‑demarcated accessory lobe of the left liver.  
   - *Pathognomonic:* None, but the combination of identical attenuation and distinct vascular pedicles is strongly characteristic.  
   - *Discriminators:* Presence of separate arterial supply and venous drainage, no internal vascularity, same liver density. Label these as *High‑importance discriminator*.

2. **Ectopic liver lobe**  
   - *Core support:* Same features as accessory lobe (isoattenuation, separate vascular supply).  
   - *Typicality:* Also typical; however, ectopic lobes usually lie separate from the main liver mass and are often found near the stomach or spleen.

All high‑importance core features (isoattenuation, distinct vascular supply & drainage, biliary duct compression) align perfectly with a classic accessory liver lobe and are incompatible with the other options. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Patient, 2 days old, born at 39 weeks of gestation from a controlled and uneventful pregnancy. At birth, notable observations included the violaceous discolouration of the scrotal skin, bilateral scrotal swelling, and difficulty in locating the testes in the scrotal sacs and inguinal canals (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: In the postnatal testicular ultrasound (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d), both scrotal sacs were occupied by heterogeneously echogenic lesions with poorly defined borders and multiple internal calcifications. These were not considered masses due to the unclear borders. Moderate increased vascularisation was noted on Doppler study.
 
 The abdominal ultrasound showed no calcifications, cysts, or free fluid. The testes and epididymides were difficult to visualise but appeared morphologically normal in the upper poles of both scrotal sacs.
 
 To complete the study, a simple X-ray of the abdomen and scrotum was performed, revealing multiple coarse, diffuse, bilateral intrascrotal calcifications, with no intra-abdominal abnormalities observed (Figure 2).
 
 MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) identified solid lesions occupying both scrotal sacs, hyperintense on T2 sequences with hypointense microcalcifications on T2. Both testes were of normal size and morphology and located within the scrotal sacs. No inguinal hernias were identified.

Clinical History: Incidental finding in the right iliac bone of a 68-year-old male patient with lung cancer who was undergoing a routine follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had lung cancer, and during a follow-up oncology CT scan, we incidentally discovered a painless mass in his right pelvic wing. CT of the pelvis shows a well-defined lesion in the right pelvis protruding into the iliac muscle with dense calcifications and a rim of fatty tissue that confirms its lipomatous nature (Figures 1a and 1b). On bone window, the calcified nature of the lesion can be clearly seen as an excrescence from the right iliac wing, causing a mild periosteal reaction on the adjacent bone cortex (Figure 2). The scout view of the CT is also helpful in observing the lesion, its close relation to the right iliac wing, with clumped calcifications and fatty tissue in the periphery (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

Clinical History: A young male presents with ataxic gait, numbness in lower limbs, and difficulty in standing and walking. To evaluate further, MR brain and MR spine screening was done, which suggests compensated hydrocephalus. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting was done to treat this. After VP shunting, he presents with headache, with symmetrical weakness in the upper limbs and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: Pre-shunt
 
 There is prominence of the midline cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space in between the lower parts of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres inferior to the vermis, displacing the vermis mildly upward and the communication to the 4th ventricle—this may represent a Blake’s pouch cyst, an incidental finding as seen in Figure 1. There is moderate dilatation of both supra and infratentorial ventricular systems without obvious periventricular ooze, i.e., the possibility of compensated hydrocephalus as seen in Figure 3. There is evidence of engorgement of the epidural venous plexus causing mass effect over the cord, as seen in Figures 4a and 4b.
 
 Post-shunting
 
 There is evidence of enlargement of the ventral epidural space showing enhancement in the cervical spine and proximal dorsal spine up to the D2 level, with extension cranially till the foramen magnum with prominent flow voids, likely engorged epidural veins within it, causing cord compression as seen in Figure 5. There is evidence of an enlarged pituitary gland (mid sagittal height of 12.7 mm) as seen in Figure 6. There is evidence of a round dural venous sinus as seen in Figure 7. Given these findings, the possibility of excessive CSF diversion needs consideration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old premenopausal woman presented with dysmenorrhea. Ultrasound (USG) revealed a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids. She subsequently underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy combined with a left salpingo-oophorectomy and right salpingectomy. Six months post-surgery, she presented with abdominal fullness and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A chest CT scan revealed numerous parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes, randomly distributed in both lungs (Figures 1a and 1b). No intralesional calcifications noted.
 
 A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identified a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass with both solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic region, the aortocaval and left external iliac regions, exerting a mass effect on nearby visceral structures without signs of direct invasion (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 A USG-guided Tru-cut biopsy was performed on the left para-aortic solid-cystic mass and sent for histopathological examination (Figure 3).
 
 On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented with complaints of right knee pain for one month and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth. No history of trauma. The patient was evaluated with X-ray of both lower limbs, computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee.
 Imaging Findings: X-rays of knee, leg and foot (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c), CT (Figures 2a and 2b) and MRI (Figures 3a and 3b) show variable bone sclerosing spectrum with shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata and melorheostosis seen in right lower limb bones involving the right femur and tibia, right fibula, multiple tarsal bones, metatarsal and phalanges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female patient was referred to the radiology department in view of a history of recurrent patellar dislocation and knee joint pain aggravated for the last 20 days. No history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: The trochlear groove appears shallow with a sulcal angle measuring 165 degrees (>150 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 1). The lateral trochlear inclination angle measures 9 degrees (<11 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 2). A trochlear facet asymmetry is seen with a ratio measuring 34% (<40% is considered abnormal) (Figure 3). The trochlear depth measures 2.5 mm (<3 mm is considered abnormal) (Figure 4).
 
 Proton density/Fat saturation (PD/FS) hyperintensity is seen in the medial patellar retinaculum with partial disruption of the fibres (<50% thickness), suggestive of a low-grade partial thickness tear. A mild lateral subluxation of the patella is seen. Grade IV chondromalacic changes (Outerbridge staging) are seen in the lateral articular surface of the patella at the patellofemoral joint space (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 17-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of markedly elevated beta-hCG levels, brown vaginal discharge, and back pain. The patient reports an uterus growing in size and can now be palpated at the level of the umbilicus. The patient has a history of irregular menses.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal sagittal (Figure 1a) and coronal (Figure 2b) uterine ultrasound shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing a heterogeneous, avascular, solid and cystic mass with multiple small cystic spaces (“bunch of grapes” appearance). Contrast-enhanced pelvic CT in sagittal (Figure 2a) and coronal (Figure 2b) projections shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing cystic and solid mass-like components.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).

**Section 1 – Candidate Extraction & Precision Mapping** - Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma  – disease entity (b) - Primary hepatic adenosarcoma  – disease entity (b) - Undifferentiated carcinoma (also known as sarcomatoid carcinoma)  – disease entity (b) - Hepatic fibroadenoma  – disease entity (b) **Section 2 – Immediate Pathognomonic Inclusion/Exclusion Sweep** No finding in the case is an absolute, pathognomonic hallmark *exclusive* to a single candidate while simultaneously contradicting all other candidates. - The presence of an adjacent dilated intrahepatic bile duct *supports* cholangiocarcinoma but does not preclude, for example, an undifferentiated carcinoma that may secondarily obstruct the bile duct. - “No enhancement” on arterial phase is *not* a definitive exclusion for cholangiocarcinoma and *does* exclude the unlikely possibility of a highly hypervascular undifferentiated carcinoma. Thus the filter does not produce an immediate single‑label answer and all candidates proceed to further analysis. **Section 3 – Feature Extraction & Attribution** | Feature | Label | |---------|-------| | Non‑enhancing low‑density liver mass (23 HU) | A (Core) | | T1‑hypointense, T2‑hyperintense signal | A (Core) | | Linear calcification at the edge of the lesion | A (Core) | | Mild shrinkage of the liver capsule | B (Strong Secondary) | | Adjacent dilated intrahepatic bile duct directly communicating with the mass | A (Core) | | Mild rim‑like enhancement of lesion edge & adjacent bile duct | A (Core) | | Slightly higher signal on DWI (b = 800) with no ADC restriction | B (Strong Secondary) | | Slight increase in FDG uptake on PET‑CT | C (Weak Secondary) | | Mild tenderness on physical examination | D (Distractor) | **Section 4 – Best‑Fit Mechanistic Synthesis, Typicality & Expert Prioritization** | Candidate | Core Features Supporting | Typicality/Variant | Pathognomonic Inclusion/Exclusion | Discriminatory Importance | |-----------|-------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------| | **Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma** | • Non‑enhancing hypovascular mass
• T1‑hypo/T2‑hyperintense
• Bile duct dilation & connection
• Edge calcification (possible)
• Mild rim‑like enhancement | Possible but variant (classic mass‑forming cholangiocarcinoma often shows delayed rim enhancement; early arterial phase may be non‑enhancing) | Inclusion: Direct bile‑duct communication is strongly characteristic of cholangiocarcinoma. Exclusion of undifferentiated carcinoma by mild FDG uptake and lack of necrosis. | High‑importance: Bile‑duct dilation & direct connection; low‑enhancement on arterial phase (expected). | | Primary hepatic adenosarcoma | None of the core features convincingly fit. | Rare; would show heterogeneous solid/cystic pattern, no bile‑duct involvement. | Exclusion: Lack of bile‑duct communication and absence of heterogenous necrosis. | Low‑importance: None. | | Undifferentiated carcinoma (sarcomatoid) | Mild DWI signal, mild FDG uptake (unlikely). | Rare; usually highly hypervascular, heterogeneous, necrotic, and strongly FDG‑avid. | Exclusion: No enhancement, no necrosis, mild FDG uptake. | Secondary discriminator: FDG uptake, enhancement. | | Hepatic fibroadenoma | None. | Uncommon benign, typically well‑defined, does not involve bile ducts. | Exclusion: Bile‑duct dilation and edge calcification inconsistent. | Low‑importance: None. | **Ranking** 1. Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma – best fit for core imaging and clinical data. 2. Undifferentiated carcinoma – excluded by lack of hypervascularity/necrosis. 3. Primary hepatic adenosarcoma – excluded by lack of biliary involvement. 4. Hepatic fibroadenoma – excluded by biliary obstruction and calcification. **Section 5 – Explicit Negative Reasoning/Exclusion Table** | Non‑selected Candidate | Core/Authoritative Criterion Against It | Explanation | |------------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------| | Undifferentiated carcinoma | Must show heterogeneous necrosis, strong FDG uptake, arterial enhancement. | Lesion is non‑enhancing, only mild FDG uptake, no necrosis on DWI/ADC. | | Primary hepatic adenosarcoma | Requires bile‑duct involvement? Not classic; shows mixed solid/cystic, lacks calcification. | No cystic component, no biliary communication, calcification atypical. | | Hepatic fibroadenoma | Should show no biliary obstruction, is typically isodense or mildly hypodense, no calcification. | Bile‑duct dilation present, calcification edge, unlikely benign. | **Section 6 – Single‑Label Selection** All high‑importance core and supporting features converge on hepatic cholangiocarcinoma. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We report a case of a 16-year-old patient living in a rural environment. The symptomatology goes back to 3 months with diffuse abdominal pain, distension, and with vomiting and fever 3 days before his admission to the ER. Clinical examination revealed a slight dullness of the flanks and hypogastrium.
 Imaging Findings: An abdominal ultrasound was performed and revealed multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion (Figure 1). Abdominal and pelvic CT scan in axial, sagittal and coronal sections showed multiple cystic lesions adherent to the peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum and mesentery (Figures 2 and 4). The largest cyst is located in the left iliac fossa, measuring 55.5 x 42 mm, containing some calcifications (Figure 3), and with a large intraperitoneal effusion. The CT scan also revealed hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 32-year-old, 12-week postpartum female presented with an incidental finding of bilaterally enlarged ovaries on ultrasound. The patient was asymptomatic at the time of presentation, with no associated abdominal pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. There was no significant personal or family history of malignancy or other chronic illnesses.
 Imaging Findings: On the initial ultrasound, both ovaries were massively enlarged (right 444 cm³, left 120 cm³) and displayed a heterogeneous echo texture with hypervascularity. No significant free fluid or adnexal torsion was noted.
 
 On contrast-enhanced whole-body MRI, bilateral ovarian masses exhibited scattered bulky lesions with significant diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement, without signs of necrosis or haemorrhage (Figure 1). Additional bulky lesions were identified in the breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and the wall of the terminal ileum.
 
 PET-CT demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (SUV max. 14 g/ml) in the same regions identified on MRI, confirming metabolic activity consistent with malignancy (Figure 2).
 
 Breast imaging (ultrasound and mammography) revealed numerous sharply circumscribed, hypervascular, hypoechoic masses in both breasts (Figure 3). The biggest lesion measured 140 cm³. Mammography confirmed these findings, identifying well-defined, radiodense masses without calcifications or architectural distortion (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Taking account of all the imaging features together, these lesions were categorised as BIRADS 5.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 9-year-old child presented with a complaint of intermittent lower abdominal pain and nocturnal enuresis for two months. There were no gastrointestinal issues, weight loss, asthenia, or lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient had no significant past medical history. During the physical examination, a palpable mass with a hard consistency and non-mobile nature was detected on the left flank. Laboratory studies were normal, including urine catecholamine levels. Tumour markers, including hCG, AFP, and CEA, were also assessed and found to be within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound revealed a solid mass inferior to the left kidney and anterior to the psoas muscle (Figure 1a). The mass was vascularised (Figure 1b), had smooth edges, was slightly heterogeneous, predominantly isoechogenic, with some echogenic foci inside, and measured 5 x 5 x 11 cm in the axial, anteroposterior, and longitudinal axes (Figure 1c).
 
 Further MRI studies (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a retroperitoneal mass inferior to the left kidney and between the left psoas and quadratus lumborum muscles. While there was no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures, it did result in anterior shifting of the psoas muscle. The lesion exhibited an intermediate and heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted images and a low signal on T1-weighted images (Figure 3), with no restriction on the diffusion study (Figures 4a and 4b). The dynamic study (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d) presented heterogeneous and progressive uptake by the contrast product, being more evident in the later phase.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male, with no prior relevant medical history, presents with atraumatic intermittent sharp pain in the lower segment of the right anterior thoracic wall. The symptoms began two months earlier when the patient started exercising (CrossFit), mostly triggered by training (especially abdominal crunches) whilst being asymptomatic in normal daily activities.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging of the costochondral region of the patient showed a normal arrangement of ribs and rib cartilage while the patient was in a supine position (Figure 1). No abnormalities in bone or cartilage contours, nor any soft tissue lesions were identified.
 
 Dynamic ultrasound of the symptomatic area, performed while the patient replicated the movement that elicits pain (abdominal crunches), revealed hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, which moved upwards and became pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage (Figures 2 and 3). This triggered the pain, with a popping sensation associated. When the patient returned to the supine position, the 9th rib returned to its normal position (Figure 4).

 Imaging Findings: Abdominal and pelvic (suprapubic) ultrasound (US) revealed an intramural uterine leiomyoma and an intrauterine device (IUD) well placed in the endometrial cavity.
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) revealed multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum (Figures 1 and 2). There are also signs of invasion of the abdominal wall. Small volume of ascites was identified in the parietocolic gutters and in the rectouterine pouch (Figures 3, 4, and 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old female presents to the emergency department with disorientation. No neurological focality is observed. The patient has a history of migraine and blurred vision in the past year. No other relevant medical background.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation in the left parietal lobe and hypointensity in the white matter (Figures 1a and 1b), which indicates a stroke lesion in the left parietal lobe. In CT with contrast (Figures 2a and 2b), stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery (arrow), a filiform course of the left middle cerebral artery and extensive collateral circulation in the region are seen (arrowheads).
 
 Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation (Figures 3a and 3b) confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side (Figures 3c and 3d) shows similar findings.
 
 FLAIR-weighted (Figure 4a) and T1 post-contrast (Figure 4b) images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).

Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2c). The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side (Figures 2a and 2b). There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging swelling over the anterior aspect of the right ankle, first noticed two years earlier. He reported no pain, trauma, functional limitation, or gait disturbance. On examination, a ~5 cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, and non-tender soft tissue mass was observed, not adherent to deep planes and without skin changes or signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon (Figure 3). The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations (Figure 3), showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences (Figure 4), and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images (Figures 5 and 6). Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.

Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented with progressive dysarthria, ataxia, gait disturbance, headache, dizziness, and a history of recurrent falls. Physical examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly without convulsions or neck rigidity. Liver enzymes were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the patient revealed symmetrical abnormal T2WI and FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral thalamus (with sparing of medial thalamic nuclei), bilateral caudate, putamen, claustrum, midbrain (with sparing of red nucleus and substantia nigra—“face of the giant panda” sign), pons and dorsal brainstem (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 2, and 3). No evidence of blooming on FFE images or restriction on DWI and ADC maps. Abnormal T1WI hyperintensity noted within the globus pallidus (Figure 4).
 
 USG of the abdomen revealed altered echotexture of the liver with hepatosplenomegaly, consistent with chronic liver parenchymal disease /(Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign) (Figure 1).
 
 Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within (Figure 2). The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse (Figure 4). PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior. Surgeons requested a contrast-enhanced CT scan to clarify the diagnosis.
 Imaging Findings: The contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed dilated small bowel loops with an apparent calibre change in the lower quadrant of the abdomen (Figure 1). As no masses or bowel thickening were identified, the diagnosis was consistent with an incomplete small bowel obstruction, likely due to abdominal adhesions—a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior.
 
 Additionally, the gastric wall showed thickened gastric folds (Figure 2). Subsequent gastroscopy confirmed the thickened folds, and biopsies were obtained (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Histopathological examination revealed atrophic gastritis and tumoural cells positive for GATA 3, mammaglobin, and oestrogen receptors.
 
 Both conventional (Figures 4a and 4b) and contrast-enhanced mammography (Figures 5a and 5b) demonstrated bilateral diffuse masses with non-mass enhancement. Palpable areas were biopsied in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast—4 o’clock—(Figure 6a) and the junction of the outer quadrants of the left breast—9 o’clock—(Figure 6b). No axillary lymphadenopathy was detected (Figures 6c and 6d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old female came with complaints of a slowly growing suprapubic mass since a few months and lower abdominal discomfort. She was further advised to undergo ultrasound imaging and subsequently CT abdomen for renal anomaly, and MRI pelvis for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: MRI pelvis reveals a large tubular T1 hyperintense structure in the pelvis on the right side, consisting of haemorrhagic contents in an enlarged hourglass-shaped right uterine horn and cervix. The left uterine horn appears unremarkable. CT abdomen (Figures 1 and 2) reveals an empty right renal fossa with a cross-fused kidney seen on the left. No obvious free fluid or adnexal lesion is seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old male with one week of sudden-onset fever, abdominal pain, cervicalgia, myalgia, palpitations and shortness of breath. Recent history of travel to Africa for 1 month, and medications for malaria prophylaxis and pre-exposure prophylaxis for HIV. On examination, he had a fever of 38,2°C, tachycardia and right lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Initial workup was non-contributory, with normal CBC, BMP, chest x-ray, and abdomen ultrasound. With wide differentials, including endemic, infectious and neoplastic conditions, CT of the head, neck and chest was performed and was unrevealing. Serology and lumbar puncture were also performed and were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, and revealed a 3.7 x 3.1 x 5.1 cm, avidly enhancing paraduodenal mass with central calcification (Figures 1a and 1b), presumed to be an enlarged lymph node. Adjacent organs, including the bowel, were unremarkable. There was no lymphadenopathy elsewhere. An endoscopic ultrasound was done (Figures 2a and 2b) to obtain an aspiration biopsy that revealed B-cell follicles with no evidence of malignancy. After discussion, the patient opted for watchful waiting. A follow-up CT done after 4 months demonstrated no significant change in size or appearance of the mass. This was subsequently excised, and histopathology revealed the final diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old woman had unintended weight loss and anaemia for several months. There was no history of bowel or urinary changes, and an endoscopy was negative. A CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed to evaluate for malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in a portal venous phase (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). An ovoid, heterogeneous soft tissue mass with smooth, well-defined margins was present in the left upper mesorectal space, mildly displacing the rectum to the right. It measured 61 x 58 x 45 mm and did not contain cystic spaces or macroscopic fat. The mass did not herniate through the sciatic notch and did not appear along the expected path of a nerve or vasculature. Upon administration of IV contrast, the mass was hypervascular on the portal venous phase. There was free fluid in the pelvis, representing simple ascites, without evidence of complexity or active bleeding. No MRI was performed since the patient was lost to follow-up after her initial CT scan and biopsy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 17-year history of diabetes mellitus presented to our institution with a 3-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms, including urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, and gross haematuria. Notably absent was acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. After hospitalisation, it was found that there were pneumaturia and faeces-like changes in the urine (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a gas-like strong echo and irregular hyperechoic stone-like mass with posterior shadowing in the bladder (Figure 2a). To exclude gynaecological diseases, a transvaginal ultrasound was performed. The right lateral wall of the bladder and the adjacent bowel were connected through a fistula. The fistula tract was about 3.8 cm long and 0.58 cm wide, characterised as a central hyperechoic and peripheral hypoechoic tract on sonogram (Figure 2b). These ultrasound findings supported the diagnosis of vesico-enteric fistula, but the specific part of the intestine could not be clarified.
 
 A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a small air-fluid level at the top of the bladder (Figure 3a). A tubular tract could be seen protruding from the right lateral wall of the bladder up to the neighbouring thickened bowel and this is filled with contrast-opacified urine (Figure 3b). A diagnosis of fistulous communication between the bladder and bowel was suggested following the findings on the excretory-phase CT scan. CT coronal scan showed faecal stones in the bladder (Figure 3c).
 
 Cystoscopy showed an oedematous right lateral wall of the bladder associated with inflammatory changes. It also revealed a fistulous tract opening with a diameter of 1 cm, with pus moss and yellow floc attached to the bladder mucosal surface. The yellow floc is the stone-like mass seen on the ultrasound. The cystoscope could be inserted via a sinus tract opening in the right lateral bladder wall, where the distal tip exhibited bifurcation. Despite attempts, the endoscope could only advance 2 cm beyond the entry point, with further progression prevented by this structural anomaly.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.
 
 A subsequent revaluation, after reviewing the patient’s radiological history, concluded that the image of a cavitated nodule (Figures 2a and 2b) corresponded to the lesion visible in the previous X-ray (Figure 1b), and proposed the differential diagnosis between cavitated lung neoplasia, rheumatoid nodule and other cavitary lesions such as tuberculosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old woman presented to the gastroenterologist with postprandial fullness and nausea persisting for one year, along with a 4 kg weight loss. She had no abdominal pain and reported normal faeces. Blood tests were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), a suspicion of a thickened stomach wall was raised.
 
 CT (Figures 2a and 2b) confirmed diffuse wall thickening and hyperaemic inner lining of the stomach with a small lumen. Lymphadenopathies were identified in the gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments. No peritoneal metastases were observed on CT, but the lack of significant intra-abdominal fat limited definitive exclusion.
 
 During gastroscopy Figure 3), insufflation of the stomach was difficult, but no focal tumoral process was noted.
 
 Initial MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) showed diffusion restriction of the stomach lining. Additionally, multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions were identified, which were confirmed via diagnostic laparoscopy. Follow-up MRI (Figures 5a and 5b) showed disease progression with increased wall thickening of the stomach in the antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb, as well as the development of bilateral ovarian lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

 Symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in corpus callosum (Figures 1a, 1b, 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5) with T1 hypointensities (Figures 1c, 2c, 3c, 4c, and 4d).
 
 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter. On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.
